story:
Janice Dell, thirty-five, an accountant, often stayed late at work because going home to an empty apartment felt pointless. She longed for love and, more than anything, feared she might never have a child. Driving home one foggy night on a two-lane road, she lost steering, and when she hit the brakes, nothing answered. Her car drifted into oncoming traffic and crashed. Her seat belt and airbag saved her. At the hospital, Dr. Elway told her she would be fine and could go home in the morning.

Later, a man about forty came to her room and introduced himself as Jason Weld—the driver of the other car. He wanted to be sure she was all right. He had no visible injuries and seemed steady and kind. He offered her a ride home when she was discharged. He returned at ten, drove her to her door, and, before leaving, asked her to dinner. She said yes.

Dates followed—many, easy and warm. After three months, over dinner, they confessed they were in love. Janice suggested they live together. Jason took her hands, hesitant. He said he loved her too, but things were not simple. He asked if she agreed there was much in life and death that people did not understand. Then he told her what he had kept secret: when their cars collided, he had died. In the next moment, he found himself standing on the road, watching the EMTs lift Janice into the ambulance. A voice—not heard but known—told him he was not ready to be taken. Until an unknown time, he could remain and function almost like a living person—able to touch and be touched, or, if he willed it, to let matter pass through him. But he would eventually be taken, and he would never know when.

Janice was shocked and called it absurd. He asked how else to explain that he had stood in her hospital room looking unhurt. She demanded proof. Jason stood, helped her up, and told her to press her hand to his chest. Her hand passed through him. She fainted. When she woke, she believed.

He said that if they lived together, marriage would follow, and he could disappear at any moment—even in the middle of a kiss, in the middle of making love. Could she live like that? She looked at him and chose love. They agreed time was their enemy; even a wedding felt too risky. Janice cried, realizing fully that she could lose him without warning. He blamed himself for her pain. She said he had rescued her from loneliness and she had no regrets. Then she asked for what she wanted most: before it was too late, she wanted his child.

Jason held her gaze and agreed. That night they made love. The next morning at breakfast, he took her hands and told her she would be a mother. As she looked into his eyes, he disappeared. He was taken.

Nine months later, Janice gave birth to a boy and named him Jason. He grew healthy and handsome, with his father’s look and voice. He would not let her forget the man she loved. Years passed. Her son went his own way; she grew old. The love that began in chance and mystery lasted inside her until the end, when she, too, was taken—perhaps at last to be with Jason again.
